ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning Providence.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Providence, then, is the care that God takes over existing things.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And again: Providence is the will of God through which all existing things receive their fitting issue.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if Providence is God’s will, according to true reasoning all things that come into being through Providence must necessarily be both most fair and most excellent, and such that they cannot be surpassed.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the same person must of necessity be creator of and provider for what exists: for it is not meet nor fitting that the creator of what exists and the provider should be separate persons.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For in that case they would both assuredly be deficient, the one in creating, the other in providing.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: God therefore is both Creator and Provider, and His creative and preserving and providing power is simply His good-will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For whatsoever the Lord pleased that did He in heaven and in earth, and no one resisted His will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: He willed that all things should be and they were.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: He wills the universe to be framed and it is framed, and all that He wills comes to pass.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: That He provides, and that He provides excellently, one can most readily perceive thus.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: God alone is good and wise by nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Since then He is good, He provides: for he who does not provide is not good.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For even men and creatures without reason provide for their own offspring according to their nature, and he who does not provide is blamed.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, since He is wise, He takes the best care over what exists.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: When, therefore, we give heed to these things we ought to be filled with wonder at all the works of Providence, and praise them all, and accept them all without enquiry, even though they are in the eyes of many unjust, because the Providence of God is beyond our ken and comprehension, while our reasonings and actions and the future are revealed to His eyes alone.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And by “all” I mean those that are not in our hands: for those that are in our power are outside the sphere of Providence and within that of our Free-will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Now the works of Providence are partly according to the good-will (of God) and partly according to permission.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Works of good-will include all those that are undeniably good, while works of permission are…….

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For Providence often permits the just man to encounter misfortune in order that he may reveal to others the virtue that lies concealed within him, as was the case with Job.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: At other times it allows something strange to be done in order that something great and marvellous might be accomplished through the seemingly-strange act, as when the salvation of men was brought about through the Cross.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: In another way it allows the pious man to suffer sore trials in order that he may not depart from a right conscience nor lapse into pride on account of the power and grace granted to him, as was the case with Paul.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: One man is forsaken for a season with a view to another’s restoration, in order that others when they see his state may be taught a lesson, as in the case of Lazarus and the rich man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it belongs to our nature to be cast down when we see persons in distress.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Another is deserted by Providence in order that another may be glorified, and not for his own sin or that of his parents, just as the man who was blind from his birth ministered to the glory of the Son of Man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again another is permitted to suffer in order to stir up emulation in the breasts of others, so that others by magnifying the glory of the sufferer may resolutely welcome suffering in the hope of future glory and the desire for future blessings, as in the case of the martyrs.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Another is allowed to fall at times into some act of baseness in order that another worse fault may be thus corrected, as for instance when God allows a man who takes pride in his virtue and righteousness to fall away into fornication in order that he may be brought through this fall into the perception of his own weakness and be humbled and approach and make confession to the Lord.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Moreover, it is to be observed that the choice of what is to be done is in our own hands: but the final issue depends, in the one case when our actions are good, on the cooperation of God, Who in His justice brings help according to His foreknowledge to such as choose the good with a right conscience, and, in the other case when our actions are to evil, on the desertion by God, Who again in His justice stands aloof in accordance with His foreknowledge.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Now there are two forms of desertion: for there is desertion in the matters of guidance and training, and there is complete and hopeless desertion.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The former has in view the restoration and safety and glory of the sufferer, or the rousing of feelings of emulation and imitation in others, or the glory of God: but the latter is when man, after God has done all that was possible to save him, remains of his own set purpose blind and uncured, or rather incurable, and then he is handed over to utter destruction, as was Judas.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: May God be gracious to us, and deliver us from such desertion.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Observe further that the ways of God’s providence are many, and they cannot be explained in words nor conceived by the mind.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And remember that all the assaults of dark and evil fortune contribute to the salvation of those who receive them with thankfulness, and are assuredly ambassadors of help.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Also one must bear in mind that God’s original wish was that all should be saved and come to His Kingdom.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it was not for punishment that He formed us but to share in His goodness, inasmuch as He is a good God.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But inasmuch as He is a just God, His will is that sinners should suffer punishment.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The first then is called God’s antecedent will and pleasure, and springs from Himself, while the second is called God’s consequent will and permission, and has its origin in us.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And the latter is two-fold; one part dealing with matters of guidance and training, and having in view our salvation, and the other being hopeless and leading to our utter punishment, as we said above.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And this is the case with actions that are not left in our hands.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But of actions that are in our hands the good ones depend on His antecedent goodwill and pleasure, while the wicked ones depend neither on His antecedent nor on His consequent will, but are a concession to free-will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For that which is the result of compulsion has neither reason nor virtue in it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: God makes provision for all creation and makes all creation the instrument of His help and training, yea often even the demons themselves, as for example in the cases of Job and the swine.
